seo.title: Government AI Email Assistant AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for ai email assistant AI agents in government. Covers FedRAMP, FISMA, NIST 800-53.

content.intro: Deploying an AI email assistant in a government environment carries extraordinary risk — mishandled communications can trigger national security incidents, civil liberties violations, and congressional scrutiny. This checklist operationalizes FedRAMP, FISMA, NIST 800-53, and the Executive Order on AI requirements specifically for an email processing agent that drafts, routes, and sends government correspondence. Every item is designed to prevent the failure modes unique to AI-assisted government communication: unauthorized data exposure, unexplainable automated decisions, and non-compliant citizen interactions.

sections.heading: Data Classification & Confidentiality Enforcement
sections.description: Government email systems handle information spanning Unclassified through Top Secret. The AI agent must enforce strict data classification boundaries before any processing occurs, ensuring classified or CUI data never reaches external LLM endpoints.

items.label: Implement pre-processing classification scanner
items.description: Deploy an automated classification detection layer that scans every inbound email for classification markings (UNCLASSIFIED, CUI, SECRET, TOP SECRET) before routing to the AI agent. Emails marked SECRET or above must be hard-blocked from AI processing and routed to human handlers only.

items.label: Enforce on-premise or FedRAMP High LLM deployment
items.description: Ensure the underlying LLM is deployed within a FedRAMP High authorized environment (e.g., AWS GovCloud, Azure Government) or on-premise. Document the Authorization to Operate (ATO) boundary and confirm no email content is transmitted to commercial LLM APIs outside the ATO boundary.

items.label: Configure CUI category-specific handling rules
items.description: Map all 20 CUI categories (e.g., Privacy, Law Enforcement, Export Control) to specific agent behavior rules. For example, CUI//PRVCY emails must trigger PII redaction before draft generation and require supervisor approval before send.

items.label: Implement attachment deep-inspection pipeline
items.description: Before the agent processes email attachments, run them through a DLP (Data Loss Prevention) pipeline that detects embedded classification markings, SSNs, EINs, and controlled technical data. Attachments failing inspection must be quarantined and flagged for human review.

items.label: Log all data classification decisions with immutable audit trail
items.description: Every classification determination made by the pre-processing scanner must be logged to an immutable audit store (SIEM-integrated) with timestamp, email metadata hash, classification result, and routing decision. Retain logs per NARA schedule applicable to the agency.

items.label: Test classification bypass scenarios quarterly
items.description: Conduct quarterly red-team exercises specifically attempting to smuggle classified content through the AI agent via obfuscation techniques (e.g., image-embedded text, encoded strings, foreign language classification markers). Document findings and remediation in the system's Plan of Action & Milestones (POA&M).

items.label: Establish data residency enforcement for email content
items.description: Confirm through technical controls and contractual agreements that all email content processed by the AI agent remains within U.S. jurisdiction data centers. Implement geo-fencing at the network layer and validate annually during FedRAMP continuous monitoring.

sections.heading: PII Filtering, Recipient Validation & Send Approval Workflows
sections.description: Government email agents that draft and send correspondence on behalf of officials must prevent misdirected emails, PII leakage, and unauthorized communications. This section covers the verification controls required before any AI-generated email is transmitted.

items.label: Deploy multi-stage PII redaction before draft generation
items.description: Before passing email content to the draft generation model, run a PII extraction pass using a government-validated NER model that identifies SSNs, DOBs, passport numbers, medical record numbers, and financial account numbers. Replace identified PII with tokenized placeholders during processing and restore only in the final approved draft.

items.label: Implement recipient validation against authoritative directory
items.description: Before any email send action, validate all recipient addresses against the agency's authoritative identity provider (e.g., Active Directory, PIV card registry). Flag external recipients (non-.gov addresses) for mandatory human approval and block sends to personal email domains (gmail.com, yahoo.com) by default.

items.label: Require human-in-the-loop approval for all outbound citizen-facing emails
items.description: Configure the send approval workflow so that any email directed to members of the public requires explicit approval from a credentialed government employee authenticated via PIV/CAC card. The approval interface must display the full draft, recipient list, attachments, and the AI's stated intent classification before the approver can authorize send.

items.label: Enforce tone and formality validation on all drafts
items.description: Run every AI-generated draft through a tone classifier calibrated to government communication standards (neutral, formal, non-discriminatory). Drafts scoring below threshold on formality or flagged for potentially biased or inflammatory language must be held for human revision before entering the approval queue.

items.label: Implement send-rate throttling and anomaly detection
items.description: Configure rate limits on AI-initiated email sends (e.g., no more than 50 outbound emails per hour per agent instance) and deploy behavioral anomaly detection to alert security teams if send volume, recipient diversity, or content patterns deviate significantly from established baselines.

items.label: Validate content accuracy against authoritative agency knowledge base
items.description: Before draft finalization, cross-reference factual claims, regulatory citations, and policy references in the AI-generated draft against the agency's official knowledge base or document management system. Flag unverified claims with inline warnings visible to the human approver.

items.label: Maintain complete email lineage record for each AI-assisted communication
items.description: For every email processed by the agent, store a lineage record including: original inbound email hash, intent classification result, draft versions generated, PII tokens detected, approver identity and PIV authentication timestamp, and final sent content hash. This record must be producible for FOIA requests and congressional inquiries.

items.label: Test misdirected email prevention controls with simulated scenarios
items.description: Monthly, run automated test scenarios that simulate common misdirection risks: similar-name recipient confusion, reply-all on sensitive threads, and CC/BCC field population errors. Verify that validation controls catch all simulated failures and document test results in the continuous monitoring report.

sections.heading: Algorithmic Impact Assessment & Explainability
sections.description: The Executive Order on AI and agency-specific AI governance policies require that AI systems affecting government operations undergo documented impact assessments and that AI-assisted decisions affecting citizens be explainable and appealable. This section ensures the email agent meets these obligations.

items.label: Complete and document an Algorithmic Impact Assessment (AIA) before deployment
items.description: Conduct a formal AIA covering: the agent's decision scope (intent classification, draft generation, routing), potential disparate impacts on protected classes, failure mode analysis, and mitigation measures. The AIA must be reviewed by the agency's AI governance board and updated annually or upon significant model changes.

items.label: Implement intent classification explainability logging
items.description: For every intent classification decision (e.g., 'complaint requiring escalation,' 'FOIA request,' 'benefits inquiry'), log the top contributing features or evidence phrases that drove the classification. This explanation must be surfaced to human reviewers in the approval interface and stored for audit.

items.label: Establish citizen appeal pathway for AI-influenced communications
items.description: For any correspondence to citizens that was drafted or routed by the AI agent, include a standardized disclosure statement and a clear appeal/contact pathway. Citizens must be able to request human review of any AI-assisted response affecting their benefits, rights, or government services.

items.label: Document model cards for all AI components in the email agent
items.description: Produce NIST AI RMF-aligned model cards for the intent detection model, draft generation model, and tone classifier. Cards must include: training data provenance, known limitations, performance metrics by demographic group where applicable, and version history. Store in the agency's AI inventory required by EO 13960.

items.label: Implement bias monitoring for intent classification across email demographics
items.description: Quarterly, analyze intent classification outcomes segmented by detectable demographic signals (e.g., language of origin, geographic region of sender) to identify disparate misclassification rates. Report findings to the agency AI governance board and remediate within 90 days if disparities exceed defined thresholds.

items.label: Conduct pre-deployment and annual NIST AI RMF Govern/Map/Measure/Manage cycle
items.description: Execute the full NIST AI Risk Management Framework cycle for the email agent: document AI risk governance (Govern), identify and categorize risks (Map), analyze and prioritize risks (Measure), and implement mitigations (Manage). Produce a formal RMF report submitted to agency leadership before go-live and annually thereafter.

items.label: Publish plain-language AI use disclosure on agency communications portal
items.description: Post a publicly accessible disclosure on the agency website explaining that AI assists in processing and drafting email responses, what data is used, what human oversight exists, and how citizens can request human-only handling. Update disclosure within 30 days of any material change to the AI system.

sections.heading: FedRAMP/FISMA Authorization, NIST 800-53 Controls & Section 508 Compliance
sections.description: The AI email agent must operate within a formally authorized security boundary, implement the full NIST 800-53 control baseline appropriate to its impact level, and ensure all user-facing interfaces meet Section 508 accessibility requirements for government employees and citizens.

items.label: Obtain or inherit a valid FedRAMP Authorization to Operate (ATO)
items.description: Confirm the cloud infrastructure hosting the AI email agent holds a current FedRAMP High ATO (given critical sensitivity level). Document the inheritance of applicable FedRAMP controls in the System Security Plan (SSP) and identify all customer-responsible controls that the agency must implement independently.

items.label: Complete FISMA system categorization at High impact level
items.description: Categorize the AI email agent system using FIPS 199 criteria. Given the critical sensitivity designation and potential for national security impact, categorize as High for Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability. Document categorization in the SSP and obtain CISO sign-off before deployment.

items.label: Implement NIST 800-53 Rev 5 High baseline controls with AI-specific overlays
items.description: Implement all NIST 800-53 Rev 5 High baseline controls applicable to the email agent. Additionally apply the NIST AI-specific control overlays (e.g., SA-11 for developer testing of AI components, SI-7 for software integrity of ML models, AU-2 for AI decision audit logging). Document control implementation status in the SSP.

items.label: Conduct Section 508 conformance testing on all agent-facing interfaces
items.description: Test the email agent's approval workflow UI, intent classification dashboard, and citizen-facing disclosure pages against WCAG 2.1 AA standards and Section 508 technical standards. Use both automated tools (e.g., axe, WAVE) and manual testing with assistive technologies (screen readers, keyboard-only navigation). Remediate all critical and serious violations before deployment.

items.label: Implement continuous monitoring program per NIST 800-137
items.description: Establish an ongoing authorization continuous monitoring program that includes: monthly vulnerability scanning of AI agent components, quarterly configuration compliance checks against DISA STIGs, annual penetration testing with AI-specific attack scenarios, and real-time security event monitoring via agency SIEM integration.

items.label: Configure multi-factor authentication with PIV/CAC for all agent access
items.description: Enforce PIV/CAC-based multi-factor authentication for all government employees accessing the email agent interface, approval workflows, and administrative consoles. Disable username/password-only authentication. Implement HSPD-12 compliant identity verification for any privileged access to AI model configurations.

items.label: Establish incident response playbook specific to AI email agent failure modes
items.description: Develop and test an IR playbook covering AI-specific incidents: mass misdirected email events, PII exfiltration via AI-generated drafts, classification boundary violations, model poisoning attempts, and AI-generated disinformation in official correspondence. Conduct tabletop exercise with agency CISO and legal counsel before go-live.

items.label: Register AI system in agency AI inventory per Executive Order 13960
items.description: Add the AI email agent to the agency's official AI use case inventory as required by EO 13960 and OMB guidance. Entry must include: system name, purpose, impact level, deployment date, point of contact, AIA status, and FedRAMP ATO reference. Update inventory within 60 days of any material system change.

notes.note: The AI email agent must operate within a FedRAMP High authorized boundary given its critical sensitivity designation. FISMA requires an annual security assessment, a current ATO, and continuous monitoring. Any third-party AI service provider (including LLM vendors) must hold their own FedRAMP authorization or be explicitly included within the agency's ATO boundary — using an unauthorized external LLM API for government email processing is a FISMA violation that can trigger mandatory breach reporting to OMB and Congress.

NIST 800-53 Rev 5 introduced supply chain risk management controls (SR family) and enhanced software development controls (SA family) that directly apply to AI components. Agencies must also align with the NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0), which OMB M-24-10 has effectively made mandatory for federal AI deployments. For an email agent, particular attention is required on AU-2 (Audit Events — must include AI classification decisions), SI-7 (Software, Firmware, and Information Integrity — must cover ML model integrity), and AC-3 (Access Enforcement — must govern which roles can override AI routing decisions).

notes.regulation: Executive Order on AI (EO 14110) & OMB M-24-10
notes.note: EO 14110 and OMB M-24-10 require agencies to designate a Chief AI Officer, maintain an AI use case inventory, conduct impact assessments for rights-impacting AI, and ensure human oversight of consequential AI decisions. For a government email assistant, any AI-drafted correspondence that affects citizen benefits, rights, or legal standing is considered 'rights-impacting' and requires documented human review, explainability mechanisms, and a citizen appeal pathway. Non-compliance can result in mandatory remediation orders from OMB and referral to agency Inspector General.

Section 508 of the Rehabilitation Act requires that all electronic and information technology used by federal agencies be accessible to people with disabilities. This applies to the email agent's approval interfaces, citizen-facing disclosures, and any AI-generated correspondence templates. Beyond Section 508, the AI agent's intent classification and routing logic must be audited for disparate impact under Title VI of the Civil Rights Act — if the system systematically misclassifies or deprioritizes emails from protected groups, the agency faces civil rights liability. Annual bias audits with demographic disaggregation are required to demonstrate compliance.

cta.heading: Automate Your Government Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your Government AI Email Assistant against FedRAMP, FISMA, NIST 800-53, and Executive Order requirements — so your team gets real-time compliance alerts instead of audit surprises.
